channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Water as a gas separation membrane
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70630-w

Ultrathin layers of liquid water confined in nanoporous membranes enable fast, selective, and stable separation of carbon dioxide from other gases.
item.dc:title: Water as a gas separation membrane
item.dc:creator: Kian P. Lopez
Max Saffer-Meng
Mohammad Allouzi
Yukai Tomsovic
Joshua N. Sherrit
Sasha R. Neefe
Patrick O. Saboe
Mou Paul
Abhishek Roy
Anthony P. Straub

item.title: Point defects in monolayer WSi<sub>2</sub>N<sub>4</sub> and MoSi<sub>2</sub>N<sub>4</sub>
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70946-7

The MoSi2N4 family is an emerging class of van der Waals materials with interesting properties. Here, the authors report a systematic characterization of point defects in monolayer WSi2N4 and MoSi2N4, showing their influence on the electronic properties of the materials.
item.dc:title: Point defects in monolayer WSi<sub>2</sub>N<sub>4</sub> and MoSi<sub>2</sub>N<sub>4</sub>
item.dc:creator: Jinmeng Tong
Yu Cao
Yuan-Kun Wang
Zhibo Liu
Dayong Yang
Chengjian He
Chuan Xu
Peitao Liu
Shao-Chun Li
Xing-Qiu Chen
Wencai Ren

item.title: Electrified interfacial oxygen-down water boosts efficient and durable electrolysis
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70737-0

The oxygen evolution reaction is hindered by slow proton transfer. Here, the authors report an ordered oxygen-down water layer that accelerates initial water deprotonation and proton transport, enabling low-voltage, durable membrane electrolysis at high current.
item.dc:title: Electrified interfacial oxygen-down water boosts efficient and durable electrolysis
item.dc:creator: Yingying Xu
Zhaoyang Shi
Shicheng Zhu
Yingxia Zhao
Ming Sun
Haozhi Wang
Yida Deng
Tianyou Zhai
Lin Yu
Youwen Liu
